The `Template` class you're looking at is internal to the Django template library. In the above code, when Django 1.7 is being used, `template` is an instance of the class you pointed out above.

In 1.8, different template languages backends ("engines") can be used and they often have their own `Template` class. Django's (DTL for short) is now just one of them. [Jinja2 has another one](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/jinja2/blob/master/jinja2/environment.py#L871) and Django supports it out of the box.

The signature of the `render()` method for each, however, is a bit different. DTL's is `Template.render(self, context)` and Jinja2's is def `Template.render(self, *args, **kwargs)`.

To provide a uniform interface, the methods on each engine's that return templates return an instance of a *different* `Template` class that is just required to have one method: `Template.render(self, context=None, request=None)`. [Here is DTL's](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/template/backends/django.py#L63) and [here is Jinja2's](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/template/backends/jinja2.py#L64).

Suppose you have the following code:

```
class ExampleTask(luigi.Task):
    param = IntParameter(batch_method=list)
```

Now `ExampleTask(1).param` is an int, `ExampleTask(2).param` is an int, and so on. But if I send these both to the scheduler and get back a batch of both of them, I get `ExampleTask([1, 2])`. `ExampleTask([1, 2]).param` is a list of ints. If I call serialize on it, it's going to fail. I'll also have to write weird code that handles both ints and lists of ints.

To make this work cleanly, I'll need to change the type of this parameter from int to list of ints. I'll need to change the parse and serialize functions. Now `batch_method=list` won't work because if I batch `ExampleTask([1])` and `ExampleTask([2])`, I get `ExampleTask([[1, 2]])`. Again, the types are wrong because `list` changes the type from `X` to `list<X>`. So I also need to import or write a flatten function to return a list.

So in order to get a `list` batch method, I need to write custom parse and serialize methods and find a flatten function. The parse and serialize methods might also need to handle weird escaping in case the parameter value could include the list separator. The only way I can see to make this all easy and likely to succeed for the average user is to provide a well-tested decorator. Otherwise, they're likely to fall into the same trap you did (and I did in some of my unit tests before getting this far) and just set `batch_method=list`.

@k4nar after some investigation, I think there are some cases we should handle. I think depending on `msgpack` is something we must have since it will be the newer version + the one in roadmap to 1.0. Unfortunately it's possible that we're changing users implementation. For instance:
* we don't pin the `msgpack-python` version so that users may do in their applications (i.e. for specific reasons they have to use version 0.4.8)
* with the current `ddtrace` package, the version will stay 0.4.8 and we're fine with it (and so the user)
* we ship a new version that depends on `msgpack` that is version 0.5.4
* users update their dependency and so in their requirements file they have both `msgpack-python` and `msgpack`
* depending on the installation order, we may overwrite their version of msgpack that now is 0.5.4 and not 0.4.8 (and it could lead to unexpected behaviors in their application)

It's something temporarily that we will change on version 1.0, but until then we're "upgrading" the dependency in an unexpected way. Of course it applies on the other way round, but in the current state we'll at most set the same version (since 0.5.4 is available also for `msgpack-python`.

`request.headers` seems to have the same API than a python `dict`.
You can thus just use `if request.headers.get('dnt')`.

If the 'DNT' header is not set, the value will be None, evaluated to false.
If the 'DNT' header is set to 0, the value will be 0, evaluated to false.
if the 'DNT' header is set to 1, true, TRUE, the value will be evaluated to true.

If so, that's different to the C implementation because during `$s` `s` will maintain its old value (and only be updated at the end) - for example, consider:

```
externals ( stem )
strings ( s t )
define stem as (
  $s = 'foo'
  $s ( = 'bar' $t = s )
)
```

With the C backend, both `s` and `t` will be set to `bar`; if the value of `s` is only updated at the end of the `$` section then `t` would instead be set to `foo`.

I'm not sure whether it makes sense to build grpc/grpcio from source here. It is significantly larger than the dependencies in python.bzl. grpc source is about 4.3 MB, as compared with the sizes of html5lib, six and werkzeug sources, which are all below 400 kB. grpc source contains C++ and Python, while the aforementioned smaller ones are mostly python. So building grpc from source would likely significantly increase the build time. In this sense,  grpcio is more similar to the depencencies: numpy and tensorflow, in that it should be directly installed from binary for the build environment. When the debugger plugin is ready for a public release, grpcio should be listed as a dependency of the tensorboard pip package (or at least stated explicitly as a requirement in the error message if it is not installed.)

In launchd you can set env vars by calling ```launchctl setenv <VAR> <VALUE>``` however this is not persisted. The only way to "persist" such a setting is to create a .plist that sets those values after booting. this has (at least) 2 disadvantages: a) execution order can't be controlled in launchd b) it creates a dependency on another plist

So I figured setting those vars inside the plist is the most hasslefree way of doing things.

> I was planning to do this later in a enhancements - redesign PR, but it looks like it wouldn't make much sense to use the old approach here if we're planning to refactor it soon anyway.

Would you prefer we don't do it soon?

> In short, I think the updates in renewal time should keep getting triggered from the interfaces.GenericUpdater.generic_updates() even if calling the enhancement interface methods to keep the changes to Certbot core code minimal when adding new enhancement types as the call chain used for renewals is complicated enough as it is.

While I acknowledge, appreciate, and agree with you that tracking discussion in GH PRs is painful, I can't stop myself from providing feedback here. I hope you don't mind too much :smile:

I actually prefer we have a separate updater method for each enhancement. First, I think this allows for better documentation because the arguments each method takes can be cleanly and traditionally documented in a function rather than trying to do something like we do with `IInstaller.enhance` where we pass a dict and try and document in another file the values in the dict for each enhancement. If you were thinking of only calling `generic_updates` once for all updates, I think having separate methods helps prevent `generic_updates` from taking too many arguments over time.

Second, by having a single function, I think it causes each plugin to implement the code calling and/or determining which updates to call. Look at Apache/Nginx's implementation of `enhance`. By putting this code in Certbot, plugins don't have to implement it, simplifying their implementation and resulting in less code.

I'm certainly open to not doing things this way, but I do have a pretty strong preference for an update method per enhancement.

I'm not sure this sleep is necessary. In most cases, I'd expect validation to occur very infrequently relative to the TTL (on the order of months vs. the order of minutes). Because records are deleted at the end of each execution, I wouldn't expect records from a previous challenge to still be cached (and if the absence of a record were being cached, that would be [determined by the TTL on the SOA record](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2308), not our `self.ttl` value here, which seems like something that would cause major problems for our workflow since SOA TTLs might be very high).

Once `_wait_for_change` has returned for each `change_id`, it seems like we should be good to ask the server to validate the challenges.

There are some edge cases around, for example, having a pool of machines behind a load balancer which each order certificates for the same domain on a similar schedule. However, that seems like a less common use case and retrying in cases like that is something we should be handling in a generic way (since the issue could occur with any DNS provider).
